desc:
Here the moors are interrupted by a large valley, and it is down into this that
the path leads. Looking down a verdant green valley can be seen, somewhat at
odds with the rest of the landscape around here. A river runs through the centre
of the valley, several hundred feet below, and it is perhaps this and the
protection from harsh winds that allows the greenery to prosper down there.
name: Top of a Treacherous Path

desc:
You are on a path at the edge of the moors, overlooking a wide and deep valley.
From here you can choose either one of the myriad of small footpaths to the
north and west, or the trail into the valley to the east. The path across the
slope leads down to a pine forest a long way below, and is rather steep in
places. It would be well-advised to pick one's footing carefully down there.
name: Overgrown Track at a Valley Edge

desc:
This oddly-coloured dirt tunnel leads further north, while a spacious cavern
opens to the south. The dirt that composes the surrounding walls and ceiling is
a deep reddish-brown colour; small trickles of water running down the walls seem
to pick up the reddish tones, creating rivulets with the appearance of blood.
The air here is cool and still, and a faint metallic smell can be detected
beneath the heavy earthy odour that fills the tunnel.
name: Tunnel of Blood-red Dirt

desc:
A solid path has been created on the soft earthen floor of the tunnel by the
continuous trampling of heavy troll feet. The tunnel walls consist mostly of
dry, crumbling dirt and small stones; a few large, gaping holes show where
larger boulders have been forcefully excavated and carried away. The rich smell
of soil has been muted by the odours of decay and faecal matter; rot and faeces
have been tracked into this part of the tunnel by the trolls and trampled into
the dirt underfoot.
name: Troll Burrow

desc:
A crude tunnel has been dug through the soil here. The walls are uneven and
appear unsteady; no bracing has been put in place to support them. A huge black
boulder to the west seems to have blocked the digging in that direction, and the
tunnel instead burrows down underneath it.
name: Troll Burrow

desc:
A wide tunnel with a rough, uneven wall has been dug out of the loose, earthy
soil. Rocks and rubble have been left along the walls, as if the work to create
this tunnel was hastily abandoned. Several large boulders have been left by the
southern wall, serving as a crude set of stairs as the tunnel ascends steeply.
name: Troll Burrow

desc:
This tunnel has been dug out of the surrounding rich, reddish-brown soil. The
soil is loose, and crumbles away from the walls and ceiling easily; various
furrows and pits in the surrounding surfaces are evidence of either natural
erosion or purposeful digging. The air here is cool and damp, with the
occasional draft coming from the east.
name: Tunnel of Blood-red Dirt

desc:
The walls of this tunnel consists of hard, densely packed earth. The southern
wall has been punched through from the other side, and loose earth lies
sprinkled on the floor around the opening. A tunnel in that direction leads into
another cavern. A large, crude slab has been put in place at the tunnel opening
to serve as an obstacle. The tunnel of packed earth continues to the west, and
to the east the tunnel leads into a ravine with jagged, steep rock walls.
name: Crossroad in the Depths

desc:
A long, narrow ravine lined by steep rocky slopes begins here and leads off into
the distance to the north. Loose rocks has fallen to the ground and lie in
scattered fragments everywhere. Towards the west, there is a tunnel opening and
a huge boulder that has been moved to this area to serve as a barricade between
this ravine and the western tunnel.
name: Underground Ravine by a Boulder

desc:
The ravine is deep and almost impassable here. The sides rise high to disappear
from view above, lost in darkness and gloom. Boulders come bouncing down the
slopes of the ravine every now and then, shattering and splintering into tinier
rocks. The slightest sound bounces back and forth between the walls, echoing up
and down the ravine.
name: Underground Ravine

desc:
This deep fissure runs through the bedrock like a terrible wound made with a
saw-edged blade. The sides are jagged and unstable, rocks and pebbles constantly
sliding down the sides to come to a rest once they reach the ravine's bottom.
Here and there large rocks lie scattered into dozens of fragments where they've
landed after a long fall from an unseen ceiling above.
name: Bend in the Underground Ravine

desc:
The cavern floor is littered with rocks and crushed boulders that have fallen
from a roof so far above that it is lost in gloom. The walls of the narrow,
steep-sided tunnel are most accurately described as a ravine. The slightest
sound travels up and down the ravine, bouncing back and forth as the echoes
multiply. A concentration of fallen rubble at the western wall speaks of past
rockfalls and the dangers of these deep caves.
name: Bend in the Underground Ravine

desc:
Sheer walls rise on both sides with no signs of places where climbing could even
be considered. The only way out of this place is to continue onwards, or head
back the way from which one came. The sounds of falling rocks echo in the ravine
intermittently, the sound travelling up and down the length of the great
fissure.
name: Underground Ravine

desc:
Three branches of a winding, narrow underground ravine meet here. A steep, harsh
slope blocks any conceivable travel towards the north. The three ravines lead
off to the south, west, and east, neither direction showing any sign of an end
of ravines or a way up.
name: Branching Underground Ravine

desc:
Landslides consisting of large boulders and jagged rocks have crashed to the
floor of the ravine here some time in the not too distant past. The ravine runs
like a great fissure through the subterranean landscape. It is slowly being
filled by the falling rocks, and one wonders if perhaps one day it will become
completely impassable.
name: Underground Ravine

desc:
Rugged, steep slopes enclose this cavern, forming a deep and almost impassable
ravine. Large rocks lie scattered across the floor of the ravine, and scree-like
formations almost completely block it in other places. Every now and then a
large rock comes tumbling down the side of the ravine, setting off a landslide
of gravel that join the accruing piles at the bottom.
name: Underground Ravine

desc:
This steep and narrow ravine leads off into the distance to the south. Here, the
two walls meet, and the ravine comes to an end. There is a breach in the western
wall of the ravine, where something punched through from the other side. A
rough, uneven hole near the corner where the ravine ends leads deeper into
whatever may be found below.
name: Underground Ravine by a Breach

desc:
This hole is located in the floor of an underground ravine. The stony ground of
the ravine above is gradually replaced by dirt towards the bottom, the shift in
the geology easily evident on the hole's walls. A tunnel through the soft earth
leads off towards the south, sloping slightly downwards further toward the roots
of the world.
name: Dank Hole

desc:
Here, a long tunnel, ten feet wide and equally tall, burrows through soft, moist
earth. The tunnel continues onwards to the south but seem to come to its end
towards the north. It requires arduous effort to travel here: the soft, moist
earth cling to ones feet and legs, not unlike walking through a mire.
name: Dank Earth Tunnel

desc:
It is unclear whether this rounded tunnel deep in the underground is a work of
nature or a burrow created by something. It may seem much too rounded and
conveniently straight to be the work of nature, but it is also inconceivable
that something would go through the trouble to dig a tunnel in this soft dirt, a
material so soft that it is likely to collapse at any time.
name: Humid Earth Tunnel

desc:
The air here is very humid, and the surrounding earthen walls are dripping wet
with moisture. Small clumps of wet earth frequently fall to the ground, joining
the ankle-deep soft mush that makes travelling here a strenuous affair. The
stench of mould and mildew is very strong, no doubt it comes from the
sickly-white filaments that cling to walls in big, putrid patches.
name: Moist Earth Tunnel

desc:
The air in this spacious cavern is hot and humid. Thin wisps of steam rise from
a dark pool, only to slowly dissipate and form condensation of the walls and
ceiling. Water drips from the ceiling at a steady pace and runs down the walls
in tiny rivulets. A narrow chute leads upward at the side of the cavern opposite
of the pool. Tunnels lead to the west and south.
name: Beside an Underground Pool

desc:
Small pools of muddy water have formed in places on the tunnel floor because the
earth is too saturated to absorb it. The tunnel itself is round and low, barely
ten feet from floor to ceiling. Clumps of wet earth fall to the ground
constantly, and the entire tunnel seems to be nothing more than a hole through
wet, soft clay just waiting to collapse.
name: Moist Earth Tunnel

desc:
The walls of this tunnel consists of wet and pliable dirt. Here and there, big
puddles of water have formed, where stale water gives off a rancid, unhealthy
smell. The tunnel continue in a straight line towards the north, while to the
south it slopes upwards.
name: Humid Earth Tunnel

desc:
The tunnel continues here through soft, moist earth. Further up the tunnel, the
wet earth gradually gives way to rocks and gravel. In some places, large rocks
have come to rest after a tumbling fall down the slope. In the opposite
direction, the tunnel leads deeper into the earth.
name: Sloping Earth Tunnel

desc:
The walls on both sides of this passage have partly crumbled and fallen inwards.
A passage has been cleared through the middle of the rubble: a wide swath of
pathway leading towards the south, and down a slanting passage to the north.
Among the rubble on the eastern side there are scraps of rotting cloth poking
through, as if something was hastily buried beneath the stones.
name: The Crumbling Passage

desc:
A huge circular grotto of black stone has been created here: a magnificent
example of nature's architecture. The ceiling is shaped like an enormous,
pitch-black dome. Large heaps of rubble have been piled against the walls to
keep passageways clear. Tunnels lead off in all directions, and judging by the
occasional crashing sounds of falling rocks, this chamber is an island of
stability in an otherwise dangerous cavern.
name: Conglomeration of Rubble

desc:
Big, sharp rocks poke up through the hard earth floor in this passageway, stuck
where they fell from the roof above. In places, holes in the floor show where
rocks has been cleared away to keep the path accessible. The walls are full of
cracks and cavities where loose rocks have tumbled down to the cavern floor.
name: The Crumbling Passage

desc:
A narrow, winding passage leads through the deep underground. Fissures and
cracks run up and down the walls, some even cross the whole arc of the cavern's
roof to meet other fissures on the opposite walls. Obviously, these walls are
under great stress, and the danger of lingering here needlessly is very clear.
Broken rocks lie scattered across the floor. Against one wall far along the
southern end of the tunnel, a huge boulder has fallen down, blocking off half of
the passageway.
name: Twist in the Crumbling Passage

desc:
The walls of this passage slope inwards, and the roof is considerably narrower
than the floor. Sheets of rock and dirt have fallen to the ground where they now
rest as piles of rubble. The centre of the corridor is surprisingly clear of
debris and easily passable. The passage leads on towards the north and south.
Occasionally, falling rocks nearby are announced by the rumble and hard cracks
of stone smashing against stone.
name: The Crumbling Passage

desc:
Dank, slimy rocks provide useful hand and footholds in this narrow chute.
Several wide, faintly glimmering veins of copper and quartz run through the
rock. A hint of warmer air can be felt in the weak updraft coming from below. A
steady drizzle of water is coming from above.
name: In a Chute

desc:
Climbing this narrow, steadily ascending shaft is easier than it may seem,
mostly because the walls are filled with niches, ledges, and jutting boulders.
Water slowly trickles down the walls, only to disappear down into the depths
below. Faintly glittering veins of minerals streak through the stone, displaying
a mixed palette of greens and yellows.
name: In a Chute

desc:
There is a slight hint of warm air rising from below. Faint wisps of mist or
steam dissipate in the air. Brown and green slime slowly oozes and trickles down
the walls, tainting the air with an odour of rot and putrefaction. A small
recess in the wall of the steeply sloping tunnel offers cover in case something
comes tumbling down the chute. A series of crude indentations in the walls make
for a makeshift ladder.
name: Recess in the Chute

desc:
A constant drip-drop of falling, dribbling, and trickling water is ever present,
the source lying somewhere further up the chute. Thin rivulets of sickly green
slime ooze down at a more leisurely pace, mixing with the water and covering
parts of the wall in a wet, glistening layer of goo. The chute snakes up and
down through the bedrock. Travel here is hard but not impossible, largely thanks
to numerous small ledges, jutting rocks, and other hand and footholds.
name: In a Chute

desc:
The walls are covered by a wet sheen of water and slime, originating from a hole
in the ceiling clogged with rotting vegetation. The slime oozes slowly along the
wall, dripping from rock to rock and gathering in miniscule pools. Downwards, a
chute leads off into the darkness. Its sides are uneven and have plenty of hand
and footholds, thereby make climbing it a fairly easy thing to do.
name: Top of a Chute

desc:
Above, this hole opens into a wide opening several dozen yards across. The sides
of the hole slope inwards, forming a funnel. At the bottom, the hole is circular
and only a few yards wide. Various vegetational detritus has fallen into the
hole, some sticking to the wet sides, but most gathering in a rotting heap at
the bottom. The decomposing plants, a mix of fungi, vines, moulds, and leaves,
is kept wet by water slowly dribbling down the hole's sides.
name: Detritus-filled Hole

desc:
These caves are a veritable marshland. The air is humid and filled with the
smells of a varied flora. Fungi, moss, and various plants seem to have found
ideal conditions in which to thrive. A large number of fractures run across the
cavern's ceiling, and through these water constantly seeps in, adding to the
marshlike and spongy cavern floor. The fractures open up to the outside world,
and although rarely more than a hand's breadth wide, they provide ample sunlight
for the plants in here to flourish.
name: Mire Caves

desc:
A wide expanse of wet, spongy bog covers most of the ground is this watery cave.
From cracks in the ceiling, tiny rivulets of water come seeping down into the
cave, making splattering noises in the pools and mud in a neverending downpour.
Various plants find the constant moisture ideal to live in, and the sparse
sunlight that finds its way down through the fissures gives them enough light in
which to thrive.
name: Subterranean Bog

desc:
This narrow niche has been converted from a stony burrow to a wet and unpleasant
morass. The muddy water is deep enough to be knee-high for a troll, and any hole
or pit beneath would be impossible to see before stepping in it. A couple of
large boulders rise from the mud, overgrown with grass and small plants. The
roof is fissured, allowing water and sunlight to find their way down here.
name: Underground Morass

desc:
The ground is moist and covered with a layer of mud, yet in places boulders rise
above most of the sodden pools. Fungi seem to find this area ideal and are
positively thriving here in a state of general abundance. The soft sounds of
dripping of water are ever present, as water falls from the cavern's ceiling,
eventually finding its way down through cracks and fractures.
name: Fungus-infested Chamber

desc:
In these caves, a wide variety of fungi and vines grow in abundance. The air is
full with the sounds of buzzing insects and dripping water. Dead vegetation rot
away in an ever deepening marsh-like mire, at places deep enough for a hobbit to
drown in. Many of the fungi that flourish here seem sensitive to being
disturbed, if the faint quivering, spurts of released spores and odd pulsating
movements are indications to be relied on.
name: Subterranean Quagmire

desc:
Dead vines and rotting vegetation float in shallow, mud-brown pools of water,
and an unpleasant odour of decay fills the air. There is a constant inflow of
water from above, where fissures in the ceiling opens up to the world outside.
The water trickles down, already filthy from whatever marsh lies above, and it
oozes down along the walls, slowly adding to the mire-like cavern floor.
name: Sodden Dead End

desc:
Piles of broken rock clutter the room, mainly at the wide cavern's western end
where an opening high up on the wall is clogged with stones of all sizes.
Choking, silvery-grey dust hangs suspended in the still air, hinting at a recent
rockslide. The cave seems to be a natural formation, and it opens up into a
wider hall to the east.
name: By a Landslide

desc:
The walls and ceiling of this small niche consist of hard but brittle grey rock.
In places, there are large clear surfaces on the walls where sheets of rocks
recently have fallen to the floor. The ground however is surprisingly devoid of
rubble, as if someone cleared it regularly. The niche opens into a much larger
chamber to the west.
name: Crumbling Niche

desc:
Cracked rocks lie scattered across the floor of this wide passage. Jagged
fissures run up and down the walls on either side of the tunnel, and dust sifts
down from cracks in the ceiling at irregular intervals. A path clear from
obstructive rubble and rocks follows the centre of the tunnel from north to
south: it is too straight to be natural.
name: Crumbling Passage

desc:
A wide section of the eastern wall has collapsed and now partially blocks this
passage. Rubble covers most of the tunnel floor, yet a surprisingly rubble-free
path runs down the centre of the tunnel. Where the rubble has been cleared, a
hard-packed dirt surface, worn down to a deeper gravel layer, is visible. Deep,
wide cracks line the walls of the tunnel, and there is no place where the rock
seems completely solid or stable.
name: Crumbling Passage

desc:
An unfinished tunnel leading in from the north comes to its conclusion here. The
excavation seems to have caused a cave-in as the southern part of the tunnel
comes to an abrupt end in a jagged hole that leads down into a deeper,
natural-looking cavern. Piles of rock are heaped against the walls, the effort
to remove them apparently abandoned.
name: Unfinished Troll Burrow

desc:
A crude, unfinished tunnel has been dug through the dark bedrock here. The walls
are uneven and edges of large boulders of harder rock jut out into the tunnel.
The effort of removing them was obviously not worth the bother. Rubble is strewn
about the tunnel and the floor is covered by a deep layer of dust and grime.
name: Unfinished Troll Burrow

desc:
A burrow has been created through the hard bedrock. Great brutal force must have
been used in the effort to create this tunnel. The walls and floor show no sign
of chiseling and minecraft. Rather, the stone has been forced to give way before
raw power with great furrows and raw wounds giving witness to the impressive
effort applied to creating this tunnel.
name: Unfinished Troll Burrow

desc:
This crudely dug-out burrow, twelve or thirteen feet in diameter, lies abandoned
and dust-covered in the depths of the mountain. Quickly-rigged wooden poles and
planks secured by old, thick ropes prevents the whole area from caving in. The
soil is dark and wet, and the air is thick and heavy. To the north, there is a
massive, black troll-door resting on five massive, rusted iron hinges. The
burrow itself continues to the south.
name: Unfinished Troll Burrow

desc:
This rounded, carved grotto is roughly twelve feet high and just about as wide.
Poles of wood brace the walls, keeping them from caving in. There is no doubt
that this excavation project is unfinished and abandoned: mouldy ropes and wet,
broken tools lie scattered about the area, arranged in a way that leaves the
impression that the excavators left in a hurry. The burrow makes its way through
the moist, hard-packed earth to the north. To the south, a huge slab of black
stone blocks further passage.
name: Unfinished Troll Burrow

desc:
The earthy ground shifts in colours of dark brown and black, some parts softer
than others. The tunnel seems to have been hastily dug out, and cracked,
half-rotten wooden beams have been put up to support the irregular ceiling and
walls. A hole in the western wall leads into a shaft, while the tunnel itself
continues to the north and south.
name: Narrow Earth Tunnel

desc:
An irregularly-shaped hole in the ceiling, neither round nor square, opens into
a dark cave above. Small ledges remain from a recent excavation, creating easy
access to that higher tunnel. The eastern exit is lined with broken planks,
tilted boulders, and loose rocks, suggesting that a barrier was smashed to
pieces.
name: Narrow Shaft

desc:
This tunnel is but a dozen feet high and wide, and tapers off to the north. It
looks much like the tunnel of an exploring badger: round, with rough edges and
no apparent purpose. Beams of dark, rotted wood support the densely-packed, wet
soil walls and ceiling, keeping them from collapsing. The tunnel continues to
the south, where it widens slightly.
name: Narrow Earth Tunnel

desc:
There is a hole in the centre of this cavern floor, opening up into a steep
shaft. The shaft is lined with ledges every three to four feet, creating a crude
yet usable semi-natural staircase. The chute is deep, and its bottom must lie
too far down to be seen from this height. Broken planks, rusting pickaxes and
other tools lie spread about the cavern, seemingly abandoned. The cave ascends
to the north.
name: Precipitous Slope

desc:
This rock-choked tunnel continues to the north and south. Huge rocks and
boulders turn the steep slope into a maze, creating a terrain that is both
difficult and painful to navigate. The deep black walls and ceilings have been
crudely dug out with little skill and in obvious haste. A faint draft can be
felt from the north, bringing cold, dry air into the tunnel. Small spiders
scurry along the dirt-packed ground, disappearing into cracks and beneath rocks
when disturbed.
name: Precipitous Slope

desc:
The black rocks that make up the floor, ceiling and walls of this tunnel feels
dry and cold to the touch. A multitude of smaller rocks lie scattered across the
cavern floor, and the walls are uneven and jagged. It looks as if the tunnel was
excavated in haste and with little skill. The dark tunnel slopes steeply
downwards to the south, steeply enough that passage that way looks perilious. At
the top of the passageway, located to the north, a bigger cave can be discerned.
name: Precipitous Slope

desc:
A dome-shaped tunnel comes to an end here in a rather narrow cavern. A lukewarm
breeze blows through the area, coming from the north and continuing through a
wide crack in the cavern's western wall. A narrow ledge just beneath the crack
gives easy access to it. A putrid odour envelops the cave, carried here by the
draft. A narrow, ascending stone tunnel works its way upwards through the large
crack in the western wall. The bedrock here is very dark and the walls are lined
with sharp, protruding edges. A carcass lies crushed under some boulders beneath
the narrow ledge.
name: Pitch-black Corridor

desc:
This narrow tunnel comes to a blunt end to the north where it punches through to
a cavern beyond. A great uneven slab of hard, grey stone has been placed at the
tunnel opening to block the passageway. The cracks between the uneven slab and
the rough walls are wide enough that a hobbit child might squeeze through in
places. The burrow itself is crudely made and of obvious trollish origin. Great
furrows on the walls give testimony to the brutal methods by which trolls
burrow.
name: Troll Burrow
